name: Parkland Hospital
description: Parkland Memorial Hospital in Dallas, Texas, is primarily known for being the hospital where President John F. Kennedy was taken after being shot in Dealey Plaza on November 22, 1963. He was pronounced dead there. Governor John Connally, also wounded in the shooting, was treated at Parkland and survived. While officially recognized as a vital medical facility, Parkland Hospital has become a focal point for numerous conspiracy theories surrounding the Kennedy assassination. These theories often center around the medical treatment Kennedy received, the alleged alterations to his body before the official autopsy, and perceived inconsistencies in the accounts of medical staff. Proponents of these theories argue that evidence suggests a cover-up, possibly involving multiple shooters or a deliberate effort to conceal the true nature of Kennedy's wounds. Mainstream historical and medical perspectives maintain that Kennedy's death was caused by the wounds inflicted by Lee Harvey Oswald, and that the medical staff at Parkland did everything possible to save his life. Discrepancies in witness testimonies and perceived inconsistencies in the medical reports are attributed to the chaotic and traumatic circumstances surrounding the event, as well as the limitations of medical technology at the time. Critics of the conspiracy theories highlight the lack of credible evidence to support claims of a cover-up and emphasize the importance of relying on verifiable facts and established scientific principles.
subtitle: Center of JFK assassination conspiracy theories

claims.claim: Kennedy was alive when he arrived at Parkland, and better medical care could have saved him.
claims.evidence: Witness testimonies from doctors and nurses who initially examined Kennedy.
claims.criticism: Mainstream medical consensus is that Kennedy sustained fatal wounds and that the medical team did everything possible under the circumstances. The severity of his injuries was such that survival was highly improbable.

claims.claim: The wounds to Kennedy's body were altered at Parkland to conceal the true nature of the assassination.
claims.evidence: Discrepancies between the initial descriptions of the wounds at Parkland and the official autopsy report.
claims.criticism: Mainstream explanations attribute these discrepancies to the chaotic environment, differing perspectives of witnesses, and the need for a thorough forensic examination during the autopsy. The autopsy report is considered the most authoritative source.

claims.claim: The Parkland doctors were part of a conspiracy to cover up the true circumstances of Kennedy's death.
claims.evidence: Allegations of conflicting statements and inconsistencies in the medical staff's testimonies over time.
claims.criticism: Mainstream historians and medical experts dismiss these allegations as unfounded and point to the pressure and trauma experienced by the medical staff in the aftermath of the assassination. Simple human error and recall bias can explain variations in testimony.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Charles Crenshaw
keyFigures.role: Author of 'JFK Conspiracy' and proponent of conspiracy theories.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Robert McClelland
keyFigures.role: Parkland doctor who observed Kennedy's wounds and later expressed doubts about the single-bullet theory.

keyFigures.name: Dr. James Carrico
keyFigures.role: First doctor to examine Kennedy at Parkland.

keyFigures.name: Gerald Posner
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Case Closed,' a definitive debunking of JFK conspiracy theories.

mythologyConnections: The death of a king/leader as a sacrifice
The search for truth and justice in the face of powerful forces

timeline.event: President Kennedy is shot in Dealey Plaza and taken to Parkland Hospital.
President Kennedy is pronounced dead at Parkland Hospital.
The Warren Commission Report is published, concluding that Lee Harvey Oswald acted alone.
Publication of numerous books and articles promoting conspiracy theories about the JFK assassination and the events at Parkland Hospital.
